preFix.title: Making a damp box for slow, even drying
preFix.subtitle: A controlled-humidity enclosure that slows clay drying and prevents cracking
preFix.excerpt: A damp box is a sealed plastic container with a wet plaster bat or damp cloth lining that maintains near-100% humidity around a clay piece during drying. Slow drying under near-constant humidity prevents the differential shrinkage that causes cracking at joins and thin sections. This technique covers how to build a damp box from common materials and how to use it at different stages of a project.
preFix.sourceNotes: Damp box construction is standard studio practice. Technique derived from first principles.

content.text: Most cracking in hand-built clay happens because different parts of the same piece dry at different rates. Thin walls lose moisture faster than thick bases. Exposed surfaces dry faster than the underside. A damp box slows the entire piece to the same rate by surrounding it with humid air. This is particularly important for large coil-built vases, assembled slab boxes, and any piece with joins between sections of different thickness. The box also keeps
greenware
workable between sessions.
When to use it

Put a piece in the damp box to slow drying when you cannot return to work on it within a few hours, when joining sections of different thickness or different drying states, and when a large piece is drying unevenly. Remove it from the damp box when you want it to progress toward
leather-hard
or
bone-dry
What you need
A large plastic storage box with a tight-fitting lid, large enough to contain the clay piece without touching the sides
A piece of plaster bat, terracotta tile, or a folded damp cloth to line the base of the box
A wooden board or a tile for the clay piece to sit on inside the box
A spray bottle of water to re-wet the lining when needed
Step-by-step
Soak a folded cloth or a plaster bat in water, then lay it in the base of the plastic box. The lining should be wet but not sitting in standing water.

Set the clay piece on a wooden board or tile elevated slightly above the wet lining, so the base of the piece does not sit directly on the wet surface. Direct contact with a wet surface makes the base too soft.
Close the lid firmly. The humid air trapped inside slows drying dramatically. A piece that would reach leather-hard in 3 hours at room temperature may take 12-24 hours in a damp box.
Check the piece daily. If condensation is dripping from the lid onto the clay, open the box slightly for an hour to lower the humidity.
Re-wet the lining cloth or plaster bat with the spray bottle when it begins to dry out. The lining is the humidity source; a dry lining stops working.
To move a piece toward leather-hard, open the lid slightly. To move it toward bone-dry, remove it from the damp box entirely and allow it to dry in open air.
What it looks like when it is working

A piece left in a well-maintained damp box overnight should look unchanged from when it was placed inside. The surface retains its colour and remains soft and workable. If you see the clay turning slightly lighter at the edges and walls while the thick base remains dark, the box is too dry: add more water to the lining.
Common mistakes
Sitting the clay directly on the wet lining: the base becomes too soft and the piece distorts under its own weight. Elevate with a board.
Letting the lining dry out: a dry cloth provides no humidity. Check and re-wet the lining daily.
Leaving a piece in the damp box for weeks: mould can develop on air-dry clay in a sealed humid environment. Remove pieces after 3-5 days and allow them to dry in stages.
